description:
You are the backup administrator for an on-premise SQLite system.  
    A production database already lives at  
        /home/user/data/company.db  

    Your job is to create a daily “cold” archive of that database and
    leave behind an auditable log of what you did.  
    Follow the exact requirements below; the automated grader will
    check them byte-for-byte.

    1.  Create the directory  
            /home/user/archive  
        if it does not already exist.

    2.  Dump the whole contents of /home/user/data/company.db to an
        SQL text file whose name embeds today’s date (UTC) in the form  

            company_YYYYMMDD.sql  

        Put that file inside /home/user/archive/.

        • Only a full sqlite3 “.dump” is acceptable – it must include  
          BEGIN TRANSACTION; the CREATE TABLE statement(s), every  
          INSERT, and COMMIT;.  
        • The file must be world-readable (chmod 644).

    3.  Package the following two items into a single tarball and then
        gzip it:

            /home/user/data/company.db
            /home/user/archive/company_YYYYMMDD.sql

        The compressed archive must be called  

            company_backup_YYYYMMDD.tar.gz  

        and stored directly inside /home/user/archive/.

        Inside the archive the two files must appear at the exact same
        paths they have on disk (i.e., the tarball root is / ).

    4.  Create /home/user/archive/backup.log **overwriting any existing
        file**.  The log must contain exactly seven lines, in the order
        shown, each followed by a single newline character “\n”, with
        no trailing blank lines and no extra spaces:

            DATE:YYYY-MM-DD
            TIME:HH:MM:SS
            EMPLOYEE_ROWS:<integer>
            DUMP_FILE:company_YYYYMMDD.sql
            ARCHIVE_FILE:company_backup_YYYYMMDD.tar.gz
            ARCHIVE_SIZE_BYTES:<integer>
            ARCHIVE_SHA256:<64-char hexadecimal hash>

        Field rules:
        • DATE and TIME are in UTC and zero-padded.  
        • EMPLOYEE_ROWS is the exact row count in table “employees”.  
        • ARCHIVE_SIZE_BYTES is the byte size of the .tar.gz file as
          reported by “stat --format=%s”.  
        • ARCHIVE_SHA256 is the SHA-256 checksum of the .tar.gz file
          (use openssl dgst -sha256 or similar; **do not** include the
          filename in the output, only the 64-character hex digest).

        Example (formatting only – do **not** copy literal values):

            DATE:2025-03-15
            TIME:09:12:04
            EMPLOYEE_ROWS:5
            DUMP_FILE:company_20250315.sql
            ARCHIVE_FILE:company_backup_20250315.tar.gz
            ARCHIVE_SIZE_BYTES:20480
            ARCHIVE_SHA256:2d711642b726b04401627ca9fbac36f5...

    5.  Verify that your archive file actually contains exactly the two
        required items.  If it does not, redo the tar/gzip step before
        finishing.

    SUCCESS CRITERIA (what the grader will check):
        • /home/user/archive exists.
        • /home/user/archive/company_YYYYMMDD.sql exists, is readable,
          and is a valid sqlite3 “.dump”.
        • /home/user/archive/company_backup_YYYYMMDD.tar.gz exists and,
          when listed with “tar -tzf”, shows exactly two entries:
              home/user/data/company.db
              home/user/archive/company_YYYYMMDD.sql
        • /home/user/archive/backup.log exists, is readable, contains
          precisely seven lines, and each field adheres to the format
          above.
        • The line “EMPLOYEE_ROWS:5” is present in the log.
        • The SHA-256 recorded in backup.log matches the actual digest
          of the .tar.gz file.

    Perform all steps through the Linux terminal.  Do not display any
    sensitive data other than what is required in backup.log.
truth:
Initial resources already present when the task starts:

    1.  File: /home/user/data/company.db  
        It is a valid SQLite database containing exactly one table:

            CREATE TABLE employees (
                id         INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
                name       TEXT NOT NULL,
                department TEXT NOT NULL,
                salary     INTEGER NOT NULL
            );

        Rows in “employees” (id, name, department, salary):
            1, 'Alice',   'Engineering', 90000
            2, 'Bob',     'Sales',       75000
            3, 'Carol',   'HR',          68000
            4, 'Dave',    'Marketing',   72000
            5, 'Eve',     'Engineering', 95000

        Thus the correct row count expected in backup.log is 5.

    2.  Permissions: /home/user and all sub-directories are owned by
        user “user”; no root/sudo access is provided.

    What the grader will look for:

    • A new directory /home/user/archive (mode 755 or stricter).  
    • SQL dump file at path  
          /home/user/archive/company_YYYYMMDD.sql  
      (YYYYMMDD == current UTC date at grading time).  The grader will
      invoke “grep -c 'INSERT INTO \"employees\"'” on this file and
      expect 5 matches.

    • Compressed archive  
          /home/user/archive/company_backup_YYYYMMDD.tar.gz  
      The grader will execute  
          tar -tzf <archive>  
      and assert that the output is exactly:
          home/user/data/company.db
          home/user/archive/company_YYYYMMDD.sql

    • Log file /home/user/archive/backup.log
        – exactly 7 newline-terminated lines, no extra whitespace.  
        – The third line must be “EMPLOYEE_ROWS:5”.

    • The SHA-256 recorded on line 7 of backup.log must equal the
      digest output of  
          sha256sum /home/user/archive/company_backup_YYYYMMDD.tar.gz
      (first field).

    All other numeric fields may vary but must be syntactically valid
    integers greater than zero.
